coredata.dc:title: A feasible method to improve the protection ability of metal by functionalized carbon dots as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitor
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Yuwei
Zhang, Dawei
Zou, Yangjun
Zhao, Haichao
Chen, Hao
coredata.dc:description: A green inhibitor of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was obtained from the reaction between citric acid and L-histidine. Then, a series of professional equipment was selected to evaluate their structure and corrosion protective behaviors in HCl solution. Quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics were chosen to discuss the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs. Electrochemical results showed that the N-CDs exhibited super corrosion protection to metal because it could form dense and ordered adsorption film on metal surface. Compared with citric acid and L-histidine inhibitors, the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs was more than 90% at 100 mg L−1. Based on the calculation of quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics, the low energy gap (ΔE = 0.0680 eV) and high binding energy (E binding = 190.85 kJ⋅mol−1) of N-CDs indicated the strong chemisorption on metal substrate, which fully explained the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs at molecular/atomic level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Electrochemistry
Adsorption
Molecular dynamics simulation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor misctext primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectrum of CA, L-His and N-CDs, (b) UV–Vis spectrum of CA, L-His and N-CDs, (c) XRF spectrum of N-CDs, (d) XPS full spectrum of CA, L-His and N-CDs, (e) XPS-C 1s spectrum of CA, (f) XPS-C 1s spectrum of L-His, (g) XPS-C 1s spectrum of N-CDs, (h) XPS-N 1s spectrum of L-His, (i) XPS-N 1s spectrum of N-CDs.
Morphology, size and dispersion of these inhibitors (a)(d)(g) CA, (b)(e)(h) L-His, (c)(f)(i) N-CDs.
Tafel curve of steel in HCl solution containing (a) CA, (b) L-His, and (c) N-CDs.
EIS data of steel in HCl solution with (a)(b)(c) CA, (d)(e)(f) L-His, and (g)(h)(i) N-CDs.
Relationship between corrosion rate and inhibitor concentration as well as immersion time (a) corrosion rate-inhibitor concentration, (b) corrosion rate-immersion time.

SEM images and EDS spectrum of Q235 steel after dipping in 0.1 M HCl without and with these inhibitors (a-1) blank, (a-2) 25 mg·L−1-CA, (a-3) 50 mg·L−1-CA, (a-4) 100 mg·L−1-CA, (a-5) 200 mg·L−1-CA, (b-1) EDS-blank, (b-2) 25 mg·L−1-L-His, (b-3) 50 mg·L−1-L-His, (b-4) 100 mg·L−1-L-His, (b-5) 200 mg·L−1-L-His, (c-1) EDS-N-CDs, (c-2) 25 mg·L−1-N-CDs, (c-3) 50 mg·L−1-N-CDs, (c-4) 100 mg·L−1-N-CDs, (c-5) 200 mg·L−1-N-CDs, (d-1) Fe, (d-2) Cl, (d-3) O, (d-4) C, (d-5) N.

LSCM images of Q235 steel after dipping in 0.1 M HCl without and with these inhibitors (a-1) blank, (a-2) 25 mg·L−1-CA-24 h, (a-3) 50 mg·L−1-CA-24 h, (a-4) 100 mg·L−1-CA-24 h, (b-1) 200 mg·L−1-CA-24 h, (b-2) 25 mg·L−1-L-His-24 h, (b-3) 50 mg·L−1-L-His-24 h, (b-4) 100 mg·L−1-L-His-24 h, (c-1) 200 mg·L−1-L-His-24 h, (c-2) 25 mg·L−1-N-CDs-24 h, (c-3) 50 mg·L−1-N-CDs-24 h, (c-4) 100 mg·L−1-N-CDs-24 h, (d-1) 200 mg·L−1-N-CDs-24 h, (d-2) 200 mg·L−1-CA-48 h, (d-3) 200 mg·L−1-L-His-48 h, (d-4) 200 mg·L−1-N-CDs-48 h.
Raman (ãc) and XPS (d) analysis of steel surface after immersion in HCl solution with (a) CA, (b) L-His, (c) N-CDs.
Langmuir isotherms for (a) CA, (b) L-His, (c) N-CDs.
The optimized geometry, LUMO and HOMO distributions of inhibitor molecules (a)(b)(c) CA, (d)(e)(f) L-His, (g)(h)(i) N-CDs.
Equilibrium adsorption configurations of inhibitor molecule on Fe (110) surface (a) Side-CA, (b) Top-CA, (c) Side-L-His, (d) Top-L-His, (e) Side-N-CDs, (f) Top-N-CDs.
Tafel parameters of all specimens in different solutions.
The electrochemical parameter obtained from EIS curve.
The calculation result obtained from quantum chemical and MD calculation.

head.ce:title: A feasible method to improve the protection ability of metal by functionalized carbon dots as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Corrosion & Protection Center, University of Science & Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Corrosion & Protection Center, University of Science & Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Prof. Jiri Jaromir Klemeš

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green inhibitor of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was obtained from the reaction between citric acid and L-histidine. Then, a series of professional equipment was selected to evaluate their structure and corrosion protective behaviors in HCl solution. Quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics were chosen to discuss the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs. Electrochemical results showed that the N-CDs exhibited super corrosion protection to metal because it could form dense and ordered adsorption film on metal surface. Compared with citric acid and L-histidine inhibitors, the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs was more than 90% at 100 mg L−1. Based on the calculation of quantum chemistry and molecular dynamics, the low energy gap (ΔE = 0.0680 eV) and high binding energy (E binding = 190.85 kJ⋅mol−1) of N-CDs indicated the strong chemisorption on metal substrate, which fully explained the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs at molecular/atomic level.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, many serious problems such as water pollution, soil pollution, toxicity and carcinogenesis are caused by heavy metals and organic pollutants (Yang et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2017; Kowsari et al., 2016a). The development of new green materials is one of the effective ways to solve these environmental crises. Carbon-based nanomaterials have been extensively applied in biological imaging, chemical sensing, photo-catalysis, cancer diagnosis and treatment fields owing to their excellent properties, such as ideal water solubility, environmental protection, wide source of raw materials, low cost, good bio-compatibility (Alibakhshi et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Samiee et al., 2019). Carbon dot is a kind of unique carbon-based nanomaterial, which has obtained great progress in anticorrosion field due to its environmental protection, low cost, simple synthesis, large specific surface area, high surface charge and adjustable function (Pham-Truong et al., 2018; Han et al., 2018; Song et al., 2017). Therefore, the application of carbon dots as corrosion inhibitor in corrosion protection has attracted widespread attention, which is a favorable candidate to replace traditional corrosion inhibitors containing heavy metals (Ye et al., 2020a).

It is well known that corrosion inhibitor protect metal through the interaction between some functional groups and the charge on the surface of metal (Cen et al., 2019a; Zheng et al., 2018; Qiang et al., 2018). In order to regulate the surface functional groups of carbon dots, the synthesis method of carbon dots can be summarized into two aspects. Firstly, the surface of carbon dots can be modified by other substances to obtain new functional groups, thereby increasing the adsorption activation energy of surface (El-Hajjaji et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Bahlakeh et al., 2019). Secondly, the structure of carbon dots can be adjusted by element doping, which is an important way to obtain functional carbon dots (Ye et al., 2020b; Cui et al., 2018; Cen et al., 2019b). Nitrogen is considered to be an excellent doping element. N-CDs can not only maintain the advantages of carbon dots, but also give them new functions. The presence of isolated electron pair in nitrogen-containing functional groups will provide potential active site, resulting in new functions and unexpected properties (Ye et al., 2019a). N-CDs contain some functional groups with heteroatoms N, and have a higher electron cloud density in the adsorption center, which can greatly reduce the corrosion rate of metals in corrosion medium (Pourghasemi Hanza et al., 2016). Cui et al. (2017) synthesized a new N-CDs through hydrothermal synthetic method, and revealed that the as-obtained N-CDs could adsorb on steel surface to isolate the corrosion medium. Ye et al. (2020c) synthesized a kind of N-CDs from natural citric acid derived material, and implied that the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs exceeded 90% at 200 mg⋅L−1.Yang et al. (2019) obtained a green N-CDs from imidazole ionic liquid and citric acid, and found that the weight loss of metal in HCl and NaOH solutions containing as-obtained N-CDs was greatly reduced.

In this work, citric acid and L-histidine were used as raw materials to obtain efficient and stable N-CDs. After that, the corrosion inhibitive behaviors of citric acid, L-histidine and functionalized N-CDs in 0.1 M HCl solution were deeply analyzed. The inhibition mechanism of functionalized N-CDs at molecular/atomic level was evaluated by density functional theory (DFT) calculation and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. This study could provide new insights into the inhibition mechanism of N-CDs and show an important guidance for widespread use.

Citric acid and L-histidine were obtained through Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Q235 steel substrates were obtained from Sheng Xin technology co. LTD, which was chosen as the test electrode. Before testing, the electrode was washed with 99.5% ethanol and burnished via a set of emery papers.

Fig. 1 showed the synthesis process of functionalized N-CDs. 1 g of citric acid and 0.1 g of L-histidine were dissolved in deionized water (50 mL) and then transferred in a volumetric flask. Subsequently, the volumetric flask was fixed in the oil bath and heated to 200 °C under reflux for 1 h. A certain concentration of nitric acid solution and sodium hydroxide solution were selected to adjust the pH of the solution in the volumetric flask. After that, the solution was purified through centrifuge with high speed rotation. The obtained substances were further purified by dialysis and PVDF membrane. A vacuum oven was selected to dry the obtained substance (N-CDs), the temperature was 65 °C and the time was 24 h. For convenience, the citric acid, L-histidine and N-doped carbon dots were abbreviated as CA, L-His, and N-CDs specimens, respectively.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) with wavenumber of 500–4000 cm−1 and Ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–Vis) with wavelength of 250–800 nm were chosen to estimate the structure of inhibitor. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with an Al Kα source was implemented to get the component information of inhibitor. During the testing, the Al Kα source was applied with 12 kV and 10 mA. X-ray fluorescence (XRF) was used to analyze the fluorescence characteristics of inhibitor and the wavelength was 300–700 nm. Scanning probe microscope (SPM) was chosen to acquire the morphology of inhibitor and the scanning area was 4 μm × 4 μm. The size and distribution of inhibitor were appraised using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at 300 kV.

Before test, 7.5, 15, 30 and 60 mg of inhibitors were added into 300 mL of 0.1 M HCl to form 25, 50, 100 and 200 mg L−1 of corrosion inhibitor solutions (test solutions). Corrosion measurement was executed using CH660 electrochemical workstation with a traditional three-electrode cell. Platinum sheet, saturated calomel, and Q235 steel electrodes were used as counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. Before each measurement, the working electrode was immersed into the corrosion inhibitor solution at a stable open circuit potential (OCP) for 30 min. Thereafter, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was achieved by imposing a 5 mV sinusoidal AC perturbation within the frequency range from 105 to 10−2 Hz at E OCP. Finally, the potentiodynamic polarization measurement was carried out at 1 mV⋅s−1 within ±250 mV versus E OCP. The electrochemical experiments were triply performed to enhance the reproducibility of data.

Prior to test, the specimen (10 mm × 10 mm × 2 mm) was polished with 400, 600, 1000 and 1500 mesh sandpapers, respectively. Subsequently, all specimens were weighed in a thermostat by a precision electronic scale. After immersion, deionized water and ethanol were selected to clean the specimen for the preparation of weight test. Then, the corrosion rate (υ corr) was acquired from NACE TM0169/G31 (Iovchev and Bankovska, 1982; Ansari et al., 2014) and the calculation equation was listed in previous study (Ye et al., 2020c).

After corrosion, deionized water was used to clean the steel surface and then nitrogen was selected to dry all specimens. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S4800) with electron gun acceleration voltage of 10 kV was used to observe the 2D corrosion morphology. Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, LSM700) was chosen to measure the 3D surface morphology of specimen.

In order to explore the relationship between the inhibition property and the electronic structure of inhibitor, DFT as a computational technique was widely used in corrosion field. Quantum chemical calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 software. LPH molecule was fully optimized at the DFT level using B3LYP method with 6–311++G(d, p) basis set. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and quantum chemical parameters were deeply studied. Meanwhile, the adsorption mode of inhibitor on the metal surface and the bonding strength were discussed. Moreover, MD simulation was an important tool to analyze the interaction between inhibitor molecules and metals. In this study, MD simulation was performed by Forcite module in Material Studio software. A simulation box containing 30 Å vacuum layer, 5 layers of Fe (110), 1 inhibitor molecule and 300 H2O molecules was used to discuss the relevant interaction.

Fig. 2 presented the result of FTIR, UV–Vis, XRF and XPS spectra. In FTIR spectrum, many obvious absorption peaks at 1185.1, 1716.9, and 3419.9 cm−1 were related to C–OH, CO, and O–H groups in CA, respectively (Fig. 2a). In the case of L-His, some different absorption peaks at 1306.9, 1642.8, 1615.8 and 2999.9 cm−1 were belonged to C–N, CN, N–H and C–H groups, respectively (Dong et al., 2012). In terms of N-CDs, a new peak corresponding to –NH–CO– was found at 1650.8 cm−1, suggesting that citric acid had reacted with L-histidine (Ye et al., 2019). In UV–Vis spectrum, no obvious peak was observed in CA and one peak was detected in L-His (Fig. 2b). After reaction, the peak was transferred from 275 to 335 nm, indicating that some nitrogen atoms were successfully doped into carbon dots. In XRF spectrum, a strong peak at 448 nm was observed in N-CDs, suggesting that the inhibitor displayed a certain fluorescence (Fig. 2c).

In XRS spectrum, the CA mainly contained C and O elements, while three elements of C, N and O were detected in L-His and N-CDs (Fig. 2d). By measurement, the N-CDs was composed of 61.3 at.% C, 11.8 at.% N and 26.9 at.% N. The C 1s spectrum of CA could be decomposed to three peaks, including C–C/CC, C–O, and CO at 284.8, 286, and 288.2 eV (Li et al., 2012), respectively (Fig. 2e). The binding energy values of C 1s spectrum in L-His were 284.9, 285.9, 287.2 and 288.1 eV, which were assigned to C–C/CC, C–O/CN, C–N and CO (Ye et al., 2019c), respectively (Fig. 2f). Similar with L-His, the N-CDs also presented four peaks of C–C/CC, C–O/CN, C–N and CO (Fig. 2g). At the same time, the N 1s spectrum of L-His was fitted to three peaks at 398.9, 399.9 and 400.9 eV, corresponding to N–H, N–C and NC (Ye et al., 2019a), respectively (Fig. 2h). Different to L-His, three peaks corresponding to pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N were found at 398.2, 399.5 and 401.2 eV in the N 1s spectrum of N-CDs (Sheng et al., 2011), respectively, implying the successful synthesis of N-CDs (Fig. 2i).

The SPM, TEM, height and size of all specimens were shown in Fig. 3 . From the result of SPM images, some agglomeration phenomena were observed on CA and L-His. By measurement, the height of CA and L-His was about 5–20 nm (Fig. 3a and b). After reaction, the N-CDs with height of 1–2 nm exhibited a uniform dispersion (Fig. 3c). The similar results were also observed in TEM images (Fig.3d ∼ f). Through statistics, the grain size of CA and L-His was about 52–62 and 60–70 nm, respectively (Fig. 3g and h). However, the grain size of N-CDs was decreased to 24–34 nm (Fig. 3i).

Fig. S1 showed the result of OCP curve. It could be seen that the OCP curves decreased firstly and then reached a stable platform. Meanwhile, the OCP value in the blank solution was lower compared to all corrosion inhibitor solutions. In addition, the OCP value was directly proportional to the inhibitor concentration. By comparison, the CA exhibited the lowest OCP value and the N-CDs showed the highest value under the same condition, demonstrating that the corrosion tendency of steel was greatly influenced by N-CDs.

The results of Tafel curves were presented in Fig. 4 . Compared with the blank specimen, the Tafel curves shifted to cathode direction after the addition of inhibitors, and this phenomenon became more visible as the inhibitor concentration increased. Moreover, the cathode portion of all Tafel curves in inhibitor solutions was parallel to the blank specimen, suggesting that the effect of corrosion inhibitor on the cathode reaction mechanism was small. In the anode portion of Tafel curves, an obvious saltation around −0.35 V was observed when steel was immersed in L-His and N-CDs solutions (Fig. 4b and c), implying that the adsorption essence of N-CDs was mainly from L-His (Qiang et al., 2017).

Corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic Tafel slope (b a), cathodic Tafel slope (b c), and inhibition efficiency (η) were listed in Table 1 . Among them, the η value was calculated from equation (1) (Amin et al., 2010; Kowsari et al., 2016b):
(1)
η =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
× 100 %
where i 0 corr and i corr represented the current densities of the uninhibited and inhibited steel, respectively.

By observation, the i corr value decreased with the addition of inhibitor, which presented a similar variation tendency to inhibition efficiency. In the meantime, the i corr value was inversely proportional to the inhibitor concentration, suggesting that the steel corrosion was greatly suppressed by increasing inhibitor concentration. By calculation, the η values reached up to 63.31%, 83.89%, 96.13% in 200 mg⋅L−1 of CA, L-His and N-CDs solutions, respectively, which implied that the N-CDs owned superior inhibition performance. Combined with previous studies, the inhibition efficiency of as-obtained N-CDs was higher than that of Salvia officinalis plant extract (87.0% at 1800 ppm) (Zheng et al., 2018), Ginkgo leaf extract (92.5% at 200 mg⋅L−1) (Qiang et al., 2018), Pyridazinium ionic liquids (84.0% at 1000 ppm) (El-Hajjaji et al., 2018), Schinopsis lorentzii extract (86.0% at 2000 ppm) (Gerengi and Sahin, 2011), Glycine max extract (67.1% at 200 ppm) (Javadian et al., 2017), 2,6-diaminopyridine (82.0% at 10 mM) (Qiang et al., 2016) and Deoxyribonucleic acid (92.0% at 40 mM) (Qiang et al., 2019). In addition, the variation of b a and b c values exhibited an uptrend compared with the blank specimen, indicating that the N-CDs were mixed-type inhibitors, which could suppress both anodic and cathodic reactions.

The result of EIS data was displayed in Fig. 5 . For Nyquist curves, the enlarged capacitive loops were observed in Fig. 5a, d, g after the inhibitor was added, this was due to the coverage of adsorbed species (Li et al., 2017; Liao et al., 2017). The diameter of capacitive loop was directly proportional to the inhibitor concentration, implying that high concentration could greatly suppress the attack of aggressive solution. This was attributed to the fact that more inhibitors could cover the corrosion region and then form an adsorption film on steel surface, thus presenting a good corrosion inhibition ability. Furthermore, all Nyquist curves displayed similar shape, indicating that the presence of inhibitor enhanced the impedance value but did not change other electrochemical characteristics in the corrosion system (Qiang et al., 2016, 2019).

In Bode plots, the |Z|
0.01 Hz was closely related to the anti-corrosion ability of material (Zhang et al., 2016; Qian et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2020d). Obviously, the |Z|
0.01 Hz presented an uptrend with augment of inhibitor concentration (Fig. 5b, e, h). After 24 h immersion, the |Z|
0.01 Hz value increased to 352, 663 and 989 Ω⋅cm2 in 200 mg⋅L−1 of CA, L-His and N-CDs solutions, respectively. This finding indicated the adsorption of inhibitor on metal surface, which effectively inhibited the serious attack of aggressive species. Meanwhile, the N-CDs showed the highest |Z|
0.01 Hz value, demonstrating the strongest protective ability to steel. Furthermore, one time constant was observed and the peak intensity increased with the augment of inhibitor concentration, implying that a drastic response was formed on the interface (Fig. 5c, f, i).

All EIS data were fitted via Zsimpwin and the equivalent circuit was shown in Fig. S2, which was made up of solution resistance (R s), polarization resistance (R p = R ct + R f), and constant-phase angle element (CPE dl). During curve fitting, CPE dl was used to replace ideal electric double-layer capacitance (C dl), which could be obtained via equation (2) (Qiang et al., 2017; Sığırcık et al., 2017):
(2)
Z
C P E
=
1
Y
( j w )
n

Among them, Y was CPE magnitude, w denoted angular frequency, j was imaginary root and n represented error parameter. Meanwhile, the C dl was calculated through equation (3) (Dehghani et al., 2019):
(3)
C =
Y 0
(
w max
)
n − 1
where w max = 2πf max and f max was the frequency at the maximum value of imaginary component in Nyquist spectrum.

The result of these parameters was shown in Table 2 . Clearly, the R s values in different solutions were stable with the change of inhibitor concentration. However, the addition of inhibitor increased the R p value, and this effect was more obvious with the augment of inhibitor concentration, suggesting that the inhibitor would inhibit the charge transfer process during corrosion process. The C dl value decreased after adding all inhibitors and then further decreased with the augment of inhibitor concentration, this was mainly due to the reduction of dielectric constant and the augment of electric double-layer thickness. Combined with previous study, the inhibitor molecules could interact with steel surface and then replace the H2O molecules at steel/solution interface (Qiang et al., 2017). Besides, the surface roughness could be reflected by the value of n (Hu et al., 2017). In general, the higher the n value is, the lower the surface roughness is. Obviously, the n value was the lowest in the blank solution and was proportion to the inhibitor concentration because the adsorbed inhibitor at the interface could decrease the surface corrosion. By comparison, the N-CDs showed the highest R p value and the lowest C dl value, implying the best anti-corrosion ability.

The variations of corrosion rate (ν corr mm⋅y−1) were presented in Fig. 6 . It could be seen that the ν corr of steel greatly reduced with the augment of CA concentration (Fig. 6a). The phenomenon caused by other inhibitors (L-His, N-CDs) also showed a similar trend. This finding revealed the successful interaction at interface, resulting in the formation of protective layer at steel/solution interface. Meanwhile, the ν corr was the lowest when the steel was immersed into the N-CDs solution, implying the best protective effect. The immersion time also affected the corrosion inhibition effect on steel. It was clear that the ν corr of steel in the blank solution was aggravated over time. Nevertheless, the opposite variation was obtained by adding three inhibitors, implying the improvement of anti-corrosion ability on steel (Fig. 6b).

2D morphology of steel after corrosion in various solutions was shown in Fig. 7 . The steel surface was seriously destroyed and exhibited very rough structure without inhibitor protection (Figs. 7a–1), while the steel surface protected by inhibitor was relatively smooth. As the inhibitor concentration increased, a smooth surface was observed because the attack of Cl− was suppressed by inhibitor. At the same concentration, the steel surface in N-CDs solution was the smoothest, that is to say, the N-CDs displayed the strongest inhibition among all inhibitors. From EDS analysis, C, O, Fe and Cl elements were found on the blank specimen (Figs. 7b–1). Nevertheless, N element was detected on the specimen treated by N-CDs, indicating the successful adsorption of inhibitor (Figs. 7c–1). At the same time, the intensity of Cl on the specimen surface after the inhibiter treatment was obviously reduced compared with the blank solution, which also confirmed its inhibition behavior. In order to explore the element distribution on the steel surface after immersion, the element scanning analysis was performed. A large number of O element was distributed on the steel surface, indicating a certain degree of oxidation reaction (Figs. 7d–3). In the meantime, N element was evenly distributed on the steel surface, illustrating the uniform distribution of N-CDs (Figs. 7d–5).

3D morphology of steel surface could provide more detailed corrosion information, which was shown in Fig. 8 . Compared with the smooth surface protected by inhibitor, the unprotected steel surface exhibited higher bumps and lower grooves. By measurement, the surface roughness of unprotected steel was about 1.971 μm, which was larger than others (Figs. 8a–1). The surface roughness was obviously reduced from the unprotected surface to the protected surface, suggesting the significant decrease of corrosion rate. For example, the surface roughness of steel in CA, L-His and N-CDs solutions was reduced from 0.933 (Figs. 8a–2), 0.902 (Figs. 8b–2) and 0.685 μm (Figs. 8c–2) at 25 mg⋅L−1 to 0.726 (Figs. 8b–1), 0.677 (Figs. 8c–1) and 0.426 μm (Figs. 8d–1) at 200 mg⋅L−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the surface roughness of steel was easily affected by immersion time. The surface roughness of steel in CA, L-His and N-CDs solutions was reduced to 0.692 (Figs. 8d–2), 0.561 (Figs. 8d–3) and 0.336 μm (Figs. 8d–4) after 48 h immersion, respectively, which was 64.89%, 71.54% and 82.95% lower compared to the blank solution. In addition, the reduction extent of surface roughness was the highest after adding N-CDs, which confirmed the analysis of electrochemical and corrosion rate. These phenomena indicated that the uniform adsorption film could be formed on steel substrate, which reduced the permeability of corrosive substance, thereby providing a good protection ability for Q235 steel.

Raman and XPS were two common techniques for appraising the type information of corrosion products. Therefore, the results of Raman and XPS data were shown in Fig. 9 . From Raman spectrum, the appearance of some characteristic peaks suggested a large amount of corrosion products on the surface of steel (Fig.9ã c). By analysis, two strong peaks at 212 and 283 cm−1 were assigned to α-Fe2O3. Besides, the peaks at 399.1, 645.2, 473.6, 554.3 and 628.4 cm−1 could be ascribed to the presence of γ-FeOOH, α-FeOOH and Fe3O4, respectively (Ye et al., 2018). By comparison, the intensity of corrosion products on steel surface was greatly suppressed with the augment of inhibitor concentration. In the meantime, the extension of immersion time also inhibited the corrosion of steel and the N-CDs presented the strongest suppression effect. For XPS spectrum, the appearance of O and Cl elements was mainly from the attack of O2 and Cl− (Fig. 9d). The content of these two elements could be reduced by adding inhibitor and prolonging immersion time, which indicated the reduction of corrosion.

Langmuir isotherm equation was well suited to analyze the adsorption type of inhibitor, which was listed as following (Qiang et al., 2015):
(4)
θ
( 1 − θ )
=
K ads
C

Among them, K ads was adsorption equilibrium constant, θ was surface coverage, and C was inhibitor concentration. Surface coverage of θ value was numerically equivalent to η value. The fitted lines and corresponding thermodynamic parameters were shown in Fig. 10 . By observation, the R
2 values of CA, L-His and N-CDs were 0.995 (Figs. 10a), 0.999 (Fig.10b) and 0.999 (Fig. 10c), suggesting that the adsorption behavior conformed to Langmuir model. The standard Gibbs free energy of
Δ
G
a d s
0
could be obtained as following (Hamdani et al., 2015):
(5)
K
a d s
=
1
C
H 2 O
exp
(
− Δ
G
a d s
0
R ’ T
)
where 1000 was molar concentration of water (g⋅L−1), R was universal gas constant, and T was absolute temperature. The K ads and
Δ
G
a d s
0
values obtained from different inhibitor solutions presented great difference. In general, the high K ads value implied a strong adsorption strength on steel surface (Sigircik et al., 2016). By measurement, the K ads value of CA, L-His and N-CDs was 17.19, 101.32 and 153.37 L⋅g−1, respectively, indicating that the N-CDs revealed the highest adsorption strength at interface. Moreover, the adsorption essence could be confirmed by
Δ
G
a d s
0
value. The
Δ
G
a d s
0
values of −20 kJ⋅mol−1 or less negative were related to physisorption, while the chemisorption could be identified from the
Δ
G
a d s
0
values of −40 kJ⋅mol−1 or more negative (Hu et al., 2016). Through calculation, the
Δ
G
a d s
0
values of CA, L-His and N-CDs were −24.16, −28.56 and −29.58 kJ⋅mol−1, respectively, demonstrating that a mixed adsorption was formed at steel/solution interface.

Based on DFT, the quantum chemical study was selected to illustrate the corrosion inhibition mechanism by analyzing the structure–reactivity correlation of organic molecules. Fig. 11 showed the optimized structure and the frontier molecular orbital (FMO) of three inhibitors. Obviously, the LUMO of all inhibitors located at partial molecular skeleton (Fig. 11c, f, i), while the HOMO spread on the whole molecular skeleton (Fig. 11b, e, h). Therefore, the whole inhibitor molecular was covered by FMO distribution, implying a flat or quasi flat adsorption of inhibitor molecule on steel surface. This finding could form a strong interaction at the interface to suppress the corrosion. It was well known that the E LUMO value represented the electron acceptance ability of organic molecules, and a low E LUMO value indicated a strong electron acceptance ability. In addition, a high E HOMO value meant a strong electron supply capability (Guo et al., 2017a; Verma et al., 2018). Therefore, the energy gap (ΔE = E LUMO − E HOMO) reflected the chemical stability of organic molecules. And a low ΔE value represented a strong adsorption strength at the metal surface (Guo et al., 2014). These values were listed in Table 3 . Compared with CA and L-His inhibitors, the lower ΔE value of N-CDs suggested that the N-CDs molecule owned higher chemical reactivity with steel surface and presented higher inhibitive efficiency.

MD simulation could provide deep insights into the interfacial interaction between inhibitor and steel, which further explained the corrosion inhibition mechanism. Fig. 12 showed the adsorption mode of inhibitors on steel surface and the corresponding equilibrium adsorption configurations on the Fe (110) surface. It could be seen that the CA was adsorbed on the surface in a warped manner (Fig. 12a and b). However, the L-His and N-CDs molecules were parallelly adsorbed on Fe surface, which could promote the release and acceptance of electron in aromatic heterocyclic molecule (Fig.12c ∼ f). Because the frontier molecular orbits were distributed throughout the whole molecule, this meant that the adsorption of these molecules on the iron matrix was accompanied by the formation of coordinate bonds. Importantly, the parallel adsorption mode could effectively control the aggressive attack by reducing the contact area between aggressive ions and iron surface (Guo et al., 2017b; Mo et al., 2017).

It is generally believed that organic molecules are adsorbed on the cathode and anode active sites of metal surface. The more negative the interaction energy (or the more positive the binding energy) between the adsorbed molecules and the iron surface is, the stronger the corrosion inhibition performance is (Amin et al., 2010). By calculation, the E binding values of CA, L-His and N-CDs were 127.12, 132.52 and 190.85 kJ⋅mol−1, respectively. Therefore, the N-CDs presented the highest E binding value, revealing the strongest adhesion strength to substrate, which was consistent with the results of electrochemical data.

The corrosion inhibitive behavior of Q235 steel in 0.1 M HCl solution containing CA, L-His and N-CDs was investigated by systematic experiment and theoretical method. Due to the forceful adsorption between N-CDs and steel surface, the N-CDs showed good protective performance to steel during corrosion process. The obtained η values of CA, L-His and N-CDs increased as the increase of inhibitor concentration, reaching up to 63.31%, 83.89%, and 96.13% at 200 mg⋅L−1, respectively. The adsorption essence of these inhibitors was chemisorption and physisorption at steel/solution interface, which was confirmed by Langmuir model. Besides, SEM and LSCM morphologies showed that the steel presented the slightest corrosion after immersion in N-CDs solution. The lowest ΔE value and the highest E binding value of N-CDs displayed the strongest adsorption on steel surface among all inhibitors, which was well explained and verified at molecular or atomic level.
Yuwei Ye: Writing - original draft. Dawei Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Yangjun Zou: Software. Haichao Zhao: Formal analysis. Hao Chen: Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully appreciate financial support provided by Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (205200100421); Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (GJJ190469); Research and Development Project of Ganzhou, Science and Technology Innovation Talents Program of Ganzhou; Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para:
